setup: HSiKAN forward+backward (sign-routed Chebyshev splines + highway gate over signed hyperedges, arity 3, d=16, cheb 6, 2 branches), 100 iters. Nagare = closed-form no-tape, now EDGE-CHUNK PARALLEL (rayon, chunk_parallel_matches_serial gate). PyTorch = same architecture with autograd. Peak RSS via /usr/bin/time -l.
single_thread_note: Per-core (1 thread): Nagare 2.3x FASTER than PyTorch (24 vs 52 ms @20k, 62 vs 140 @50k, 124 vs 283 @100k). Memory 4-5x LESS (86 vs 459 MB, 207 vs 843, 405 vs 1468).
parallel_note: hsikan_forward/backward now partition the T hyperedges into min(threads,T) contiguous chunks run on the rayon pool; checksum identical across thread counts. At 16 threads Nagare scales 7.3x (61->8.3ms @50k) while PyTorch saturates ~6-8 threads (51->42ms). Matched 16 threads: Nagare ~5x FASTER + ~3x LESS memory. Parallelism costs P-fold transient grad_x copies: RSS 196->269 MB (50k), 386->568 MB (100k) from 1->16 threads -- still 2.6-3.1x under PyTorch.

kato15_32core.note: Nagare scales 165->32ms 1->32 threads (5.2x) then SATURATES 16->32 (34->32). PyTorch REGRESSES catastrophically at 32 threads (160->5360ms @50k, 33x slowdown = CPU intra-op over-subscription thrash on tiny d=16); killed the 100k th=32 run. Nagare best (32ms) vs PyTorch best (160ms @8thr) = 5x; at matched 32 threads 167x. Nagare degrades gracefully, PyTorch collapses.

scenario_A_dim_sweep.setup: Mac, 50k edges, fwd+bwd, 1->16 thread speedup (min of 2-3 runs)
scenario_A_dim_sweep.finding: Scaling is 7.3-7.9x across d=16/32/64 at <=16 threads -- roughly FLAT in d. This REFUTES the earlier 'memory-bandwidth bound at d=16' assertion: up to 16 threads it scales cleanly regardless of d. The kato15 16->32 flattening is a separate 32-core regime whose cause is NOT isolated (bandwidth / per-chunk work too small at 32 chunks for d=16 / NUMA / turbo all plausible) -- hypothesis, not conclusion.

scenario_B_deploy_vs_train.setup: Mac, 50k edges, d=16, 8 threads, min of 2-3 runs; RSS via /usr/bin/time -l
scenario_B_deploy_vs_train.finding: DEPLOY (forward-only): Nagare 16 MB vs PyTorch 469 MB = 29x less memory (streaming chunked forward drops each chunk's cache), 1.9x faster. TRAINING: 3.7x faster. Backward OVERHEAD: Nagare +29% (closed-form, nearly free), PyTorch +150% (tape replay). The no-autograd-tape thesis pays off most on the deploy memory axis.
